title: Hypokalemia & IV Potassium Replacement
subtitle: 70 y/o male on a loop diuretic with a potassium of 2.9
nursesNote: 0800 - 70 y/o male admitted with a heart failure exacerbation, on IV furosemide and oral digoxin. He reports generalized weakness, tired legs, and muscle cramps since yesterday. The morning basic metabolic panel shows a potassium of 2.9 mEq/L. Telemetry shows frequent premature ventricular contractions (PVCs) and flattened T waves with U waves. The provider orders IV potassium chloride (KCl) replacement. Urine output has been about 45 mL/hr.

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select the FOUR (4) findings that BEST indicate hypokalemia.

opts.text: Potassium 2.9 mEq/L
opts.rat: A potassium below 3.5 mEq/L confirms hypokalemia; 2.9 is significantly low and affects the heart and muscles.

opts.text: Generalized weakness and muscle cramps
opts.rat: Muscle weakness and cramps are classic neuromuscular signs of hypokalemia.

opts.text: Flattened T waves with U waves on telemetry
opts.rat: Flattened T waves and the appearance of U waves are characteristic ECG changes of hypokalemia.

opts.text: Frequent premature ventricular contractions (PVCs)
opts.rat: Low potassium increases cardiac irritability and can cause PVCs and other dysrhythmias.

opts.text: Digoxin level 1.4 ng/mL
opts.rat: The digoxin level is within range; it is not itself a sign of hypokalemia (though hypokalemia raises digoxin-toxicity risk).

opts.text: Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL
opts.rat: Normal renal function is reassuring for replacement, but it is not a sign of hypokalemia.

opts.text: Urine output about 45 mL/hr
opts.rat: Adequate urine output is a safety check before giving potassium, not a hypokalemia sign.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: Interpret the findings and the risk
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) statements that correctly interpret the situation.

opts.text: The loop diuretic (furosemide) is a likely cause of the potassium loss
opts.rat: Loop diuretics increase renal potassium excretion and commonly cause hypokalemia.

opts.text: IV potassium must be DILUTED and infused slowly-never given by IV push
opts.rat: IV potassium given undiluted or by rapid push can cause fatal cardiac arrest; it is always diluted and infused slowly via a pump with monitoring.

opts.text: The low magnesium can make the hypokalemia harder to correct until it is also treated
opts.rat: Hypomagnesemia promotes ongoing potassium loss; potassium is often refractory until magnesium is replaced.

opts.text: IV potassium can safely be given by rapid IV push to correct the level quickly
opts.rat: This is dangerous and false-rapid IV push of potassium can cause cardiac arrest and death.

opts.text: Hypokalemia has no effect on a client taking digoxin
opts.rat: Hypokalemia increases the risk of digoxin toxicity, so it is especially important to correct.

opts.text: These ECG changes and the weakness require no action
opts.rat: The dysrhythmias and neuromuscular symptoms of hypokalemia require prompt, careful correction.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Most likely explanation for the low potassium
steps.inst: Drag to rank the explanations from MOST likely (1) to LEAST likely (5).

opts.text: Potassium loss from the loop diuretic (with contributing low magnesium)
opts.rat: A loop diuretic plus low magnesium best explains ongoing potassium loss and the current level of 2.9.

opts.text: Inadequate dietary potassium intake
opts.rat: Poor intake can contribute, but the diuretic is the dominant driver here.

opts.text: Gastrointestinal losses (vomiting/diarrhea)
opts.rat: A possible cause of hypokalemia, but none are reported in this client.

opts.text: Acute kidney injury causing potassium retention
opts.rat: Kidney injury tends to RAISE potassium; the creatinine is normal, so this does not fit.

opts.text: Laboratory error with a truly normal potassium
opts.rat: Least likely: the ECG changes and symptoms match the low value, so it is unlikely to be a lab error.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: Select the FOUR (4) MOST appropriate interventions for safe IV potassium replacement.

opts.text: Administer the KCl DILUTED in IV fluid, infused slowly via an infusion pump-never by IV push
opts.rat: Diluting the potassium and infusing it slowly on a pump is the mandatory safe method; IV push is never done.

opts.text: Keep the client on continuous cardiac (telemetry) monitoring during replacement
opts.rat: Cardiac monitoring detects dysrhythmias from either the hypokalemia or too-rapid correction.

opts.text: Confirm adequate urine output / renal function before and during the infusion
opts.rat: Verifying kidney function and urine output prevents dangerous potassium accumulation (hyperkalemia).

opts.text: Monitor the IV site for pain/irritation and recheck the potassium (and magnesium) level
opts.rat: IV potassium irritates veins, and rechecking levels confirms the correction is working and not overshooting.

opts.text: Give the potassium by rapid IV push to raise the level quickly
opts.rat: Rapid IV push of potassium can cause fatal cardiac arrest and is never done.

opts.text: Administer concentrated undiluted KCl directly into the vein
opts.rat: Undiluted concentrated potassium is extremely dangerous and prohibited; it must be diluted.

opts.text: Increase the furosemide dose to help the client feel better
opts.rat: More diuretic would worsen the potassium loss.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Drag to rank the actions from FIRST (1) to LAST (5).

opts.text: Verify the order, the diluted premixed concentration, and adequate urine output/renal function
opts.rat: FIRST-confirming a safe, diluted preparation and kidney function prevents a dangerous error before anything is infused.

opts.text: Place the client on continuous cardiac monitoring
opts.rat: SECOND-monitoring must be in place before and during potassium replacement.

opts.text: Start the diluted KCl infusion on an infusion pump at the ordered controlled rate
opts.rat: THIRD-the infusion is delivered slowly and precisely by pump, never pushed.

opts.text: Monitor the IV site, ECG, and the client's symptoms during the infusion
opts.rat: FOURTH-ongoing monitoring detects site irritation or cardiac changes early.

opts.text: Recheck the potassium and magnesium levels after the infusion
opts.rat: LAST-repeat labs confirm the correction and guide any further replacement.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: After replacement, classify each finding as Improving, Worsening, or Unchanged.

opts.text: Repeat potassium is 4.0 mEq/L
opts.rat: A potassium back within 3.5-5.0 shows the replacement corrected the deficit.

opts.text: T waves normalize and the PVCs resolve on telemetry
opts.rat: Resolution of the ECG changes reflects correction of the hypokalemia.

opts.text: Muscle strength returns and cramps resolve
opts.rat: Improved neuromuscular symptoms indicate the potassium level is restored.

opts.text: Repeat potassium is 6.2 mEq/L with new peaked (tall, tented) T waves
opts.rat: Over-correction to hyperkalemia with peaked T waves is dangerous and requires stopping potassium and treating the high level.

opts.text: New bradycardia with a widening QRS complex develops
opts.rat: Bradycardia and a widening QRS suggest hyperkalemia affecting cardiac conduction-a medical emergency.

opts.text: The IV site becomes red, swollen, and painful
opts.rat: Redness, swelling, and pain indicate phlebitis/infiltration from the irritating infusion, requiring the site to be changed.

opts.text: Repeat potassium remains 3.0 mEq/L and magnesium is still low
opts.rat: A persistently low potassium with untreated low magnesium shows the deficit has not corrected-magnesium must also be replaced.

opts.text: Blood pressure remains 120/70 as before
opts.rat: A stable, unchanged blood pressure is neither an improvement nor a decline.
